quotes.verbatim_quote:
In the keys of F, Ao and Bé major, simply take three pieces of manuscript paper, and transpose Lesson No. 4 to
each key. After you have done this and you are sure that they are right, practice each example until you know
them all by heart in each key.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Operationalises the transposition mandate as a concrete manuscript-paper exercise rather than a mental abstraction.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Now for melody chord progressions.
We are still working in the keys of F, G, A’ and Bé. We will work with the keys of C, Dé and Ee later.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Marks the pivot from rhythm comping to melody-chord playing while preserving the four-key training scope.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Let’s work on simple introductions first. Try these examples in G major. You may find some of these very hard to
play, but if you practice them chromatically up and down the finger board you will soon become accustomed to them.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Reapplies the chromatic-up-the-neck drill specifically to intros, treating physical familiarity with the shape as the path to fluency.

quotes.verbatim_quote: They are your most important melody chords for these four keys. So give them all the attention you possibly can.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Designates the Lesson 6 intro shapes as the canonical melody-chord vocabulary for F, G, A♭ and B♭.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Another way to get new ideas is to change the rhythm of the introduction. Try playing them in arpeggio style,
or try playing the bottom note of the chord first, then the chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces the rhythmic-displacement trick (root-then-chord, arpeggiation) as Baker's go-to method for generating new intros from a fixed chord set.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Another thing you must do, is to try making other intro’s by changing these around in as many ways as you can.
This may not be so easy at first, but if you devote enough time to it, you will find yourself making all kinds of
intro’s from these few chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Authorises combinatorial recomposition of a small chord set as the engine of unlimited intro-writing.

quotes.verbatim_quote: In this Lesson I have a few ideas on how to use the Arpeggio, and the string bass.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names the two characteristic accompaniment textures (arpeggio, walking string-bass) that the book elevates above plain strumming.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Using Introduction No. 2 in Lesson No. 6 work them out and try to make up new ones.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Pins the arpeggio/string-bass studies to a specific intro template, giving the student a concrete object on which to apply the texture variations.
